mappings.rationaleAI: Mendelian genetics including laws of segregation and independent assortment (A.2), molecular mechanisms of DNA replication, transcription, and gene regulation (B.2), and population genetics including gene pool and Hardy-Weinberg principle (C.1).
Chromosomes and genes as the cellular basis of heredity (B.1) and the chemical nature of genes, genetic code, mutations, and gene expression (B.2).
Transmission of genetic information from parents to offspring through Mendel's laws (A.2) and the combined action of heredity and environment in producing an organism (A.3).
Evolutionary change through natural selection, genetic drift, and mutation acting on gene frequencies (C.1) and natural selection as an agent of evolutionary change (C.2).
Natural selection as the primary agent of evolutionary change through differential survival and reproduction shifting trait frequencies (C.2).
DNA as the double-helical polymer encoding genetic information, with replication, transcription, and chromatin organization as molecular genetics fundamentals (B.2).
Darwin's formulation of natural selection as the agent of evolutionary change (C.2), later synthesized with Mendelian genetics in the modern evolutionary synthesis.
Domestication as artificial selection for genetic improvements of animals and plants (C.3), affecting behaviour genes and morphology genes across generations.
The dog as the first domesticated species, illustrating artificial selection for genetic improvements including reduced aggression and enhanced human social cue interpretation (C.3).
Maize domesticated from teosinte ~9,000 years ago, exemplifying artificial selection for genetic improvements in cultivated plants (C.3).
Sheep domesticated ~10,000 years ago from wild mouflon, illustrating artificial selection for wool, meat, and milk production in domesticated animals (C.3).
Cattle independently domesticated twice from aurochs, with over 1,000 breeds resulting from artificial selection for meat, dairy, and draft power (C.3).
The chicken domesticated ~8,000 years ago and specialized into broiler and laying breeds through artificial selection (C.3).
The pig independently domesticated twice in the Neolithic, exemplifying artificial selection for genetic improvements in domesticated animals (C.3).
The central dogma of DNA replication, transcription to RNA, and translation to protein as the chemical and molecular nature of genes (B.2).
RNA as a single-stranded nucleic acid bridging genetic information storage and protein synthesis, relevant to the expression and regulation of genes (B.2).
Proteins as the functional products of gene expression via translation, relevant to the genetic code and its expression (B.2).
Sexual reproduction enabling genetic recombination and inheritance from both parents, relevant to the gene pool and changes in gene frequencies (C.1).
Reproduction as the mechanism transmitting hereditary information (A.3) and sexual reproduction generating genetic variation within the gene pool (C.1).
Genetic engineering and recombinant DNA as applications of molecular genetics (B.2).
Species as interbreeding populations relevant to the gene pool concept and Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in population genetics (C.1).
Intelligence as a trait influenced by the combined action of heredity and environment (A.3), relevant to the nature-nurture debate in behavioral genetics.
Learning shaped by genetics, epigenetics, and environmental factors, illustrating the combined action of heredity and environment (A.3).
Cereals domesticated in the Neolithic across multiple centers, exemplifying artificial selection for genetic improvements in cultivated plants (C.3).
Potato domesticated 7,000-10,000 years ago with over 5,000 cultivated varieties, illustrating artificial selection for genetic improvements in cultivated plants (C.3).
The Scientific Revolution's empirical methods provided the intellectual foundation for early speculations on the nature of heredity (A.1), preceding Mendel's formalization.
